content:
5 min read
Just now

--

“It works on my machine.”
If you’ve been in software long enough, you’ve either said this or heard it. It’s become a running joke in the industry — there are memes, stickers, t-shirts. Somebody once jokingly suggested we just ship the developer’s laptop as the production server.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

But behind the joke is a genuinely frustrating problem. Your code runs perfectly in development. You test it, it passes, you deploy it — and within minutes something is broken in production that you cannot reproduce locally.
This isn’t bad luck. It happens for specific, predictable reasons. And once you know what those reasons are, you can stop most of them before they ever reach production.
Reason 1: Your Environment Variables Are Different
This is the most common culprit, and the most invisible one.
On your local machine, you have a .env file (hopefully not committed to Git) with values like:
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://localhost:5432/myapp_dev
API_KEY=test_key_abc123
DEBUG=true
MAX_WORKERS=2In production, those same variable names exist — but the values are completely different. The database URL points to a real server. The API key is a live key with real…
